Description: Model-View-Controller separating data (Model) presentation (View) and logic (Controller). Laravel's default architecture
Structure:
app/Http/Controllers/ — Controllers
app/Models/ — Eloquent Models
resources/views/ — Blade Views
routes/web.php — Web Routes
routes/api.php — API Routes
database/migrations/ — Schema
tests/ — Tests
When To Use: Most Laravel web applications; CRUD apps; rapid development; prototypes
When Not To Use: Large enterprise apps with complex business logic that doesn't fit in controllers or models
Key Principles: Thin controllers; fat models or services; convention over configuration

Description: Adds a Service layer between Controllers and Models for business logic. Controllers delegate to services
Structure:
app/Http/Controllers/ — HTTP handling only
app/Services/ — Business logic
app/Models/ — Data access
app/DTOs/ — Data Transfer Objects
app/Exceptions/ — Domain exceptions
tests/Unit/Services/ — Service tests
When To Use: When business logic is too complex for controllers; when logic is shared across controllers
When Not To Use: Simple CRUD with no business rules beyond validation
Key Principles: Single Responsibility; controllers only handle HTTP; services are framework-agnostic

Description: Abstracts data access behind repository interfaces. Services depend on repositories not Eloquent directly
When To Use: When you might switch ORMs; for highly testable code; when data access logic is complex
When Not To Use: Small projects where Eloquent alone is sufficient; when you only have simple CRUD queries
Key Principles: Depend on abstractions; repository per aggregate root; service layer above repositories

Description: Organize code around business domains with rich domain models. Bounded contexts isolate domain logic
Structure:
src/
  Domain/
    Order/
      Models/Order.php — Aggregate Root
      Models/OrderItem.php — Entity
      ValueObjects/Money.php
      Events/OrderPlaced.php
      Repositories/OrderRepositoryInterface.php
      Services/OrderService.php
      Exceptions/InsufficientStockException.php
    User/
      Models/User.php
      ValueObjects/Email.php
  Application/
    Order/Commands/ — Use Cases
    Order/Handlers/
  Infrastructure/
    Persistence/EloquentOrderRepository.php
    External/StripePaymentGateway.php
When To Use: Complex business domains; enterprise apps; when business logic is the core differentiator
When Not To Use: Simple CRUD apps; prototypes; when the team is unfamiliar with DDD concepts
Key Principles: Ubiquitous language; bounded contexts; aggregates guard invariants; value objects for immutability

Description: Core business logic has no framework dependencies. Ports define interfaces; adapters implement them
Structure:
src/
  Domain/ — Pure PHP business logic
    Ports/
      In/CreateOrderUseCase.php — Driving port
      Out/OrderRepositoryPort.php — Driven port
      Out/PaymentGatewayPort.php — Driven port
    Models/
    Services/
  Infrastructure/
    Adapters/
      In/Http/OrderController.php — HTTP adapter
      In/Console/ImportCommand.php — CLI adapter
      Out/Persistence/EloquentOrderRepository.php
      Out/Payment/StripeAdapter.php
When To Use: When framework independence matters; for long-lived enterprise systems; when you need to swap adapters
When Not To Use: Small apps where framework coupling is acceptable; rapid prototyping
Key Principles: Domain has zero framework imports; all I/O goes through ports; adapters are interchangeable

Description: Concentric layers with dependencies pointing inward. Entities at center use cases around them
Structure:
src/
  Entities/ — Business objects (no framework deps)
  UseCases/ — Application business rules
    CreateOrder/
      CreateOrderRequest.php
      CreateOrderResponse.php
      CreateOrderInteractor.php
  InterfaceAdapters/ — Controllers Presenters Gateways
  Frameworks/ — Laravel Eloquent external libs
tests/
  Unit/UseCases/
When To Use: When strict separation of concerns is critical; for highly testable systems
When Not To Use: When the overhead of many layers is not justified; small teams with simple apps
Key Principles: Dependency rule: inner layers know nothing about outer; use cases orchestrate entities

Description: Separate the read (query) and write (command) models for different optimization strategies
Structure:
app/
  Commands/
    CreateOrderCommand.php
    CreateOrderHandler.php
  Queries/
    GetOrderQuery.php
    GetOrderHandler.php
  ReadModels/ — Denormalized for fast reads
  WriteModels/ — Normalized for consistency
When To Use: When read and write performance requirements differ significantly; for event sourcing
When Not To Use: Simple CRUD where read and write models are identical
Key Principles: Commands mutate state; queries read state; separate optimization per side

Description: Organize a monolith into independent modules with clear boundaries. Each module has its own models services and routes
Structure:
modules/
  Order/
    Config/
    Routes/api.php
    Http/Controllers/
    Models/
    Services/
    Database/Migrations/
    Tests/
  User/
    Config/
    Routes/api.php
    Http/Controllers/
    Models/
    Services/
    Database/Migrations/
    Tests/
app/ — Shared kernel
When To Use: When you want microservice benefits with monolith simplicity; preparing for future extraction; large teams
When Not To Use: Small apps; when modules don't have clear boundaries
Key Principles: Modules communicate via interfaces not direct model access; shared kernel for cross-cutting

Description: Each business capability is a separate deployable service communicating via APIs or message queues
Structure:
service-order/
  app/ — Full Laravel app
  Dockerfile
  docker-compose.yml
service-user/
  app/ — Full Laravel app
  Dockerfile
service-payment/
  app/ — Full Symfony app
  Dockerfile
gateway/
  nginx.conf — API Gateway
When To Use: Very large teams; independent scaling requirements; polyglot technology needs
When Not To Use: Small teams; when network complexity outweighs benefits; when you need strong consistency
Key Principles: Each service owns its data; async communication preferred; API gateway for routing

Description: Design the API contract first then implement. All clients consume the same API
Structure:
routes/api.php — API routes
app/Http/Controllers/Api/V1/ — Versioned controllers
app/Http/Resources/ — JSON Resources
app/Http/Requests/ — Validation
openapi.yaml — API specification
app/Http/Middleware/ — Auth rate-limit CORS
tests/Feature/Api/ — API tests
When To Use: SPA backends; mobile app backends; third-party API providers
When Not To Use: Server-rendered apps where Blade templates are primary; internal tools
Key Principles: API versioning; consistent response format; OpenAPI spec as source of truth

Description: State changes are captured as domain events. System reacts to events asynchronously
Structure:
app/
  Events/ — Domain events
  Listeners/ — Event handlers
  Projections/ — Read model builders
  Sagas/ — Long-running processes
config/
  event-sourcing.php
database/
  migrations/ — Event store + projections
When To Use: When audit trail is critical; complex workflows with multiple reactions; when you need temporal queries
When Not To Use: Simple CRUD; when eventual consistency is unacceptable; small apps
Key Principles: Events are immutable facts; projections build read models; sagas coordinate multi-step processes

Description: Run PHP functions as serverless units on AWS Lambda or similar platforms
Structure:
handler.php — Lambda handler
serverless.yml — Serverless config
app/
  Http/Controllers/ — Request handlers
resources/views/ — Blade views

# Or Laravel Vapor:
vapor.yml — Vapor deployment config
When To Use: Auto-scaling APIs; event-driven processing; cost optimization for variable traffic
When Not To Use: Long-running processes; WebSocket connections; when cold start latency is unacceptable
Key Principles: Stateless functions; use managed services for state (RDS DynamoDB S3); minimize cold start

Description: Support multiple tenants (customers) in a single application with data isolation
Structure:
app/
  Models/Tenant.php
  Http/Middleware/IdentifyTenant.php
  Traits/BelongsToTenant.php
config/tenancy.php

# Database per tenant:
database/tenant-migrations/
# Or shared database with tenant_id:
database/migrations/
When To Use: SaaS applications; when each customer needs isolated data; white-label platforms
Key Principles: Tenant identification via domain/subdomain/header; global scope for data isolation; tenant-aware migrations

Description: Traditional layered architecture separating presentation business and data access layers
Structure:
app/
  Presentation/ — Controllers Views API Resources
    Http/Controllers/
    Http/Resources/
    Console/Commands/
  Business/ — Services Actions Rules
    Services/
    Actions/
    Rules/
  DataAccess/ — Repositories Models Queries
    Repositories/
    Models/
    QueryBuilders/
When To Use: Teams familiar with traditional layered architecture; standard business applications
When Not To Use: When layers become too coupled or when more flexibility is needed
Key Principles: Each layer only depends on layers below it; no skipping layers; clear interface between layers

Description: Design your application to be extensible through plugins or extensions without modifying core code
Structure:
app/
  Core/ — Core application
  Contracts/
    PluginInterface.php
    HookInterface.php
  Plugins/
    PluginManager.php
    PluginRegistry.php
plugins/ — Third-party plugins
  my-plugin/
    plugin.json — Metadata
    src/MyPlugin.php — Implementation
When To Use: CMS platforms; extensible frameworks; marketplace applications
When Not To Use: Applications that don't need extensibility; when maintainability is more important
Key Principles: Plugins implement interfaces; core provides hooks; plugin manager handles lifecycle
